name: Bubba Tom's Eastern North Carolina Style Barb
recipeInstructions:
While nothing can duplicate the sweet ambrosia of slow, pit-cooked, whole hog Eastern North Carolina barbeque, this is a close backyard approximation for those exiled in distant, heathen regions of barbeque heresy.
First, get pork shoulders or Boston Butt roasts that your smoker will hold comfortably. Use a Brinkmann Professional Pit Smoker with an offset firebox, or a vertical Brinkmann water smoker. The key is moist, smoky, indirect heat for a long period.
Put a bag of charcoal in the firebox, open the vents, light it, and let it burn down to coals. Add wood (generally oak) - two parts wet to one part dry - regulate dampers, and place shoulders or butts, fat side up, in the cooking chamber. Put a drip pan half-filled with apple cider vinegar beneath the meat.
Keep heat between 180-260 degrees throughout; the optimum range is 220-240 degrees. Add apple wood to the firebox as needed, and between 5-7 whole heads of garlic during the process. Keep the firebox fed and smoke going for 8 to 10 hours without opening the cooking chamber unless temperature spikes above 260 degrees.
When smoking is done, the meat will have a golden amber to dark brown crust. Put meat in a covered Dutch oven. Preheat indoor stove's oven to just under 300 degrees or raise the cooking chamber's temperature if it's too dark outside.
Make pan sauce: Fill a quart-sized Mason jar halfway with apple cider vinegar, add one (or more) teaspoons of red pepper flakes, and fill the rest with water. Pour into Dutch oven with pork, cover, and cook until meat falls from bone, about 2 hours.
Let meat cool slightly before handling it safely. Pack barbeque into a large frying pan, dissolve salt in warm water, add apple cider vinegar and red pepper sauce to the pan, turn heat to medium, and simmer frequently until sauce oozes over spatula when pressed. Remove from heat and enjoy your Eastern North Carolina Style Barbeque.
